corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: This situation poses a conceptual challenge to statistical learning theory as traditional measures of model complexity struggle to explain the generalization ability of large artificial neural networks.
A promising direction is to consider the workhorse algorithm, stochastic gradient descent (SGD), and inspect which solution SGD converges to.
So while this minimum-norm intuition may provide some guidance to new algorithm design, it is only a very small piece of the generalization story.
So while this minimum-norm intuition may provide some guidance to new algorithm design, it is only a very small piece of the generalization story.
We focus on two image classification datasets, the CIFAR10 dataset and the ImageNet ILSVRC 2012 dataset.
The CIFAR10 dataset contains 50,000 training and 10,000 validation images, split into 10 classes.
The ImageNet dataset contains 1,281,167 training and 50,000 validation images, split into 1000 classes.

Specifically, our experiments establish that state-of-the-art convolutional networks for image classification trained with stochastic gradient methods easily fit a random labeling of the training data.

We corroborate these experimental findings with a theoretical construction showing that simple depth two neural networks already have perfect finite sample expressivity as soon as the number of parameters exceeds the number of data points as it usually does in practice.
Deep artificial neural networks often have far more trainable model parameters than the number of samples they are trained on.
A satisfying answer to this question would not only help to make neural networks more interpretable, but it might also lead to more principled and reliable model architecture design.
A satisfying answer to this question would not only help to make neural networks more interpretable, but it might also lead to more principled and reliable model architecture design.
Simply applying a Gaussian kernel on pixels and using no regularization achieves 46% test error.
In the experiment on ImageNet, we use the Inception V3 architecture and reuse the data preprocessing and experimental setup from the TENSORFLOW package.
In this work we presented a simple experimental framework for defining and understanding a notion of effective capacity of machine learning models.
The detailed architecture is illustrated in Figure .
